english: 1. dry fodder for horses and other animals, as hay, barley etc.; čhag-yžoṅ (chag gzhong) trough, manger, crib. - 2. the fourth finger Med. - 3. resp. for shoe Glr., also pýag(-lhám) (phyag(-lham)). - 4. čhag-péb-pa (chag pheb pa) Glr. = pýag péb-pa (phyag pheb pa). - 5. the breadth of a fist, čag gaṅ (chag gang) id., Mṅg. frq. - 6. v. čág-pa (chag pa).
piece, fragment Lex., Thgy.; *čag-túm-la soṅ* (*chag tum la song*) C. it has gone to pieces.
Sch.: 'having only one purpose, pursuing but one aim; unremitting, indefatigable'.
(or pýag-ga-pýog-gé? (phyag ga phyog ge?)) various things mixed up or thrown together, medley.

I. 1. with byed-pa (byed pa), °debs-pa ('debs pa), to sprinkle, besprinkle, čhus (chus) with water, káṅ-pa (khang pa), lám-rnams (lam rnams) the house, the streets B., C. (W. *čab-čáb* (*cab cab*)). - 2. Sch.: čhag-čhág ɣdab-pa (chag chag gdab pa) to starch, to stiffen. II. W. čhag-čág čò-če* (chag chag co ce) to tread, to trample, e.g. the narrow paths or furrows between garden-beds; to clap the hands.

1. a large tuft or bunch of flowers, ears of corn etc. - 2. pf. of °čhág-pa ('chag pa), broken; ma-čhág(s)-pa (ma chag(s) pa), and esp. adv. ma-čhág(s)-par (ma chag(s) par) also čhág-med-par (chag med par) uninterrupted, unintermitting; uninterruptedly; gas-čhag-méd (gas chag med) without a crack, flaw, or chink. - 3. lam čhág-pa (lam chag pa) v. °čhág-pa ('chag pa).
broken; a broken vessel, pot etc., a pot-sherd; tsel-(po) čhág(-po) (tshel (po) chag (po)) a broken dosser or pannier.
diminutive of čhág-pa (chag pa), a little bunch.
bunch, °brás-bu čhág-mo ('bras bu chag mo) a fruit growing in the form of bunches or clusters, like the grapes of the vine, the berries of the elder etc. W.
a small grain, e.g. of ground grits, *čhág-tse-čàn* (*chag tse can*) granulous; *bág-pe čhág-tse-čàn* (*bag pe chag tse can*) ground grits, W.; Hind. soojee.
Sch.: the right measure, dug ster čhag-tsád (dug ster chag tshad) if a sufficient quantity of poison has been administered to a person, Med.
a wooden splint for a broken limb, *čug-čè* (*cug ce*) to put it on W.

I. frq. for čhág-pa (chag pa) 2. II. vb. to be begotten, produced; ma-čhágs-pa (ma chags pa) not begotten or produced in the usual way of propagation, but = rdzús-te skyés-pa (rdzus te skyes pa), or lhún-gyis grúb-pa (lhun gyis grub pa) Pth. frq.; mṅál-du čhágs-pa (mngal du chags pa) to be produced in the womb, as the foetus is; hence čhágs (chags) in compounds: animal, °dab-čhágs ('dab chags), yśog-čhágs (gshog chags) winged animal, bird; srog-čhágs (srog chags) in general: a living being, an animal, = séms-čàn (sems can); °prúl-gyi tsul-čhágs ('phrul gyi tshul chags) Glr. prob. as much as a wonderful child, a prodigy; śiṅ-la čhágs-pa (shing la chags pa) to grow on a tree, of fruits; and in general: to rise, arise, spring up, originate, of the world, of new works, buildings, empires, customs, of eruptions on the skin; *zíl-pa čhágs soṅ* (*zil pa chags song*) W. dew has fallen; to come forth, to appear, = °byúṅ-ba ('byung ba), e.g. °ód-du čhágs-pa ('od du chags pa) to come to light, to appear Mil.; *ṅul čhágs* (*ngul chags*) W. sweat comes forth, breaks out, I perspire; even: ráb-tu čhágs-pa (rab tu chags pa) = ráb-tu °byúṅ-ba (rab tu 'byung ba) to become a cleric (little used); čhágs-rábs (chags rabs) genesis, history of the beginning, esp. of the world; čhágs-tsúl (chags tshul) 1. manner of beginning, origin, pro-creation Med. 2. W. form, figure, demeanour, *čhágs-tsúl sóg-po* (*chags tshul sog po*) coarse, rude, rough. III. 1. vb. to love, (ἐρᾶν), bú-mo-la (bu mo la) a girl; skyés-pa daṅ na-čhúṅ yčìg čhágs-pa (skyes pa dang na chung gcig chags pa) the mutual affection between a man and a maiden; tender attachment in general, connubial, parental and filial love, yíd-la čhágs-pai bú-mo-rnams (yid la chags pai bu mo rnams) my dearly beloved daughters Pth.; ardent desire or longing
